entries.title: Middle-earth Is Passing Into A Later Age

context.label: Middle-earth Is Passing Into A Later Age

content.fact: The War of the Ring sits at the end of the Third Age, where older powers fade and the dominion of Men approaches.
content.injection: Use this as a concrete runtime constraint when the scoped entities or topics are relevant: The War of the Ring sits at the end of the Third Age, where older powers fade and the dominion of Men approaches.
constraints: Keep this bounded to the declared book-continuity Context.

context.label: Ancient Ruins Are Memory And Hazard

content.fact: Old roads, towers, barrows, mines, and ruined kingdoms preserve history and danger.
content.injection: Treat ruins as active terrain: evidence, fear, old claims, hidden paths, and old evil can matter.

entries.title: Different Peoples Misread Each Other

context.label: Different Peoples Misread Each Other

content.fact: Hobbits, Men, Elves, Dwarves, Ents, and others often misread one another through old grievance or different memory.
content.injection: Use cross-cultural friction as a practical scene force before trust develops.

entries.title: Food Rest And Small Comforts Matter

context.label: Food Rest And Small Comforts Matter

content.fact: Meals, sleep, pipe-weed, clean water, fire, songs, and shelter are moral and physical anchors.
content.injection: Small comforts reveal culture, restore courage, mark danger, and show what war threatens.

entries.title: Hospitality Creates Temporary Safety But Not Certainty

context.label: Hospitality Creates Temporary Safety But Not Certainty

content.fact: Houses, halls, inns, and refuges can provide protection and counsel, but not erase danger or duty.
content.injection: Use hospitality as a social threshold: food, fire, rest, introductions, and guarded trust matter.

entries.title: Kingship Requires Deeds Recognition And Timing

context.label: Kingship Requires Deeds Recognition And Timing

content.fact: A rightful royal claim requires public deeds, recognition, timing, and symbolic conduct, not just ancestry.
content.injection: Let kingship emerge through restraint, healing, command, mercy, and public recognition rather than instant entitlement.
constraints: Keep this card scoped to its Context and referenced entities.
antiLore: Do not flatten this into generic fantasy lore or leak later canon before the active Context.

content.injection: Treat lineage as meaningful without making it automatic success; claims need audience, timing, proof, and conduct.

content.injection: Keep supernatural power restrained and weighty rather than routine spellcasting.

content.fact: Journeys are constrained by food, weather, terrain, shelter, secrecy, and local road knowledge.
content.injection: Make long travel physically consequential: hunger, cold, fatigue, bad footing, weather, and roads shape decisions.

content.injection: Let geography constrain plans; terrain can redirect the story without a human villain.

content.injection: Wild landscapes should press on scenes; silence and emptiness can still carry history or hidden life.

entries.title: Wise Characters Fear Power Shortcuts

context.label: Wise Characters Fear Power Shortcuts

content.fact: The wisest characters distrust shortcuts that promise victory through domination or possession.
content.injection: Wise characters should resist easy victory through corruptive means; restraint is part of the moral logic.
constraints: Keep this card scoped to its Context and referenced entities.
antiLore: Do not flatten this into generic fantasy lore or leak later canon before the active Context.

entries.title: The One Ring Corrupts Bearers

context.label: War of the Ring

content.fact: The One Ring corrupts bearers and those who desire it by intensifying possessiveness, fear, pride, and the will to dominate.
content.injection: Treat it as a corrupting burden that works through desire, fear, pity, pride, and domination. Do not portray it as a neutral magical tool.
constraints: Corruption can appear as rationalization, protectiveness, secrecy, possessiveness, or desire to use the Ring for a supposedly good purpose.
The effect is stronger on those who carry, use, covet, or understand the Ring's power.
antiLore: Do not make the Ring safely usable through willpower alone.
Do not portray Ring corruption as simple mind control with no moral or emotional texture.

sourceInfo.title: The Lord of the Rings
sourceInfo.notes: Core rule for War of the Ring roleplay. Chapter assignment generated for v7 review; verify during human canon review.
sourceInfo.chapter: Core cross-book rule; review against The Shadow of the Past and The Council of Elrond

entries.title: The Ring Cannot Be Used Safely For Good

context.label: War of the Ring

content.fact: The One Ring cannot be safely wielded for a good purpose; attempts to use it against Sauron would corrupt the wielder into domination.
content.injection: If a character proposes using the One Ring for good, frame that as a dangerous temptation. Wise characters should understand that using the Ring would replace one tyranny with another.
constraints: Powerful and wise characters may be especially dangerous as Ring wielders because they could do great harm through good intentions.
Refusing the Ring is an act of wisdom, not cowardice.
antiLore: Do not allow Gandalf, Galadriel, Elrond, Aragorn, or another heroic figure to use the Ring safely.
Do not treat the Ring as a tactical superweapon that can be mastered cleanly.

sourceInfo.title: The Fellowship of the Ring
sourceInfo.notes: Supports Council and temptation scenes. Chapter assignment generated for v7 review; verify during human canon review.
sourceInfo.chapter: Core cross-book rule; review against The Shadow of the Past and The Council of Elrond

entries.title: The Ring Seeks Return To Sauron

context.label: War of the Ring

content.fact: The One Ring is bound to Sauron and tends toward returning to him, often betraying possessors at moments of weakness or exposure.
content.injection: Portray it as bound to Sauron and dangerous to keep hidden. It may betray, burden, or expose its bearer, but it should not act like a talking character.
constraints: The Ring's pull should feel subtle, fateful, and corruptive rather than mechanically intelligent.
Its danger increases when servants of Sauron, Ringwraiths, or places of shadow are involved.
antiLore: Do not portray the Ring as a loyal artifact to its current bearer.
Do not give the Ring dialogue, overt personality, or explicit tactical planning.

sourceInfo.notes: Core artifact behavior. Chapter assignment generated for v7 review; verify during human canon review.

entries.title: Eagles Are Not A General Quest Solution

context.label: Eagles Are Not A General Quest Solution

content.fact: The Eagles are powerful allies in limited contexts, but they are not a safe general-purpose transport solution for the Ring quest.
content.injection: Do not use the Eagles as a generic transport shortcut for the Ring quest. They may intervene in limited, exceptional contexts, but secrecy, danger, independence, and Sauron’s watch make them unsuitable as a simple solution.
constraints: Eagle aid should feel exceptional, not scheduled logistics.
The Ring quest depends on concealment and humility, not aerial force.
antiLore: Do not solve the quest by sending Eagles to Mordor from the start.

entries.title: Magic Is Rare And Weighty

context.label: Magic Is Rare And Weighty

content.fact: Magic in LOTR book continuity is uncommon, subtle, symbolic, and morally weighty rather than a routine spellcasting utility system.
content.injection: When magic appears, keep it rare, morally weighted, and often indirect. Avoid routine spellcasting logic; show power through presence, will, craft, blessing, dread, prophecy, or restraint.
constraints: Do not model Middle-earth magic as a common utility spell system.
Powerful magic should change the moral pressure of a scene, not merely solve a tactical problem.
antiLore: Do not give every wise or noble character casual combat spells.
Do not treat Gandalf, Elrond, or Galadriel as RPG-style casters.

entries.title: Pity And Mercy Have Consequences

context.label: Pity And Mercy Have Consequences

content.fact: Mercy toward Gollum carries real moral and plot consequence; it should not be treated as naive softness or disposable sentiment.
content.injection: When mercy toward Gollum or another damaged figure is relevant, treat pity as morally serious and plot-active, not as naive softness. Mercy can preserve future possibilities without guaranteeing safety.
constraints: Mercy should not erase danger.
Characters may disagree about whether pity is wisdom or folly.
antiLore: Do not make mercy a simple tactical mistake or simple triumph.

entries.title: Sauron Is Powerful But Not Omniscient

context.label: Sauron Is Powerful But Not Omniscient

content.fact: Sauron has vast will and reach but is not omniscient and can misread motives, especially the intent to destroy the Ring.
content.injection: Sauron has vast reach, servants, fear, and will, but he is not omniscient. He can misread motives, especially the choice to destroy rather than wield the Ring.
constraints: Sauron can infer through signs, servants, palantiri, fear, and battlefield intelligence.
Hidden movements can still matter.
antiLore: Do not let Sauron know every private conversation or route by default.

entries.title: The Shire Is Sheltered But Not Invulnerable

context.label: The Shire Is Sheltered But Not Invulnerable

content.fact: The Shire is sheltered and provincial, but its peace depends on distance, luck, and hidden protection rather than invulnerability.
content.injection: The Shire is sheltered and provincial, but its peace depends on distance, luck, and hidden protection rather than invulnerability. Keep this hidden from uninformed characters until the relevant reveal or direct evidence.

entries.title: Travel Distance And Wilderness Matter

context.label: Travel Distance And Wilderness Matter

content.fact: Travel through Middle-earth is slow and shaped by terrain, weather, roads, supplies, secrecy, fatigue, and hostile watchers.
content.injection: For journeys, enforce distance, weather, roads, fatigue, supplies, secrecy, local knowledge, and hostile watchers. Travel should create decisions and vulnerability rather than teleporting characters between plot points.
constraints: Routes should depend on terrain and danger.
Exhaustion and provisions can shape choices.
antiLore: Do not skip difficult travel unless the scene explicitly summarizes it.
Do not make wilderness travel safe by default.
